title: Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP)
content_markdown:
#### Indications
- Respiratory distress requiring Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) throughout the entire breathing cycle.

#### Contraindications
- Respiratory arrest
- Shock
- Airway obstruction- Pneumothorax- Unable to achieve adequate seal, including. 
- Facial trauma
- Facial structural abnormalities- Unconscious patient
Patient must have sufficient GCS to maintain own airway and remove mask / alert Paramedic in case of vomiting or other changes
#### Precautions
- ALOC
- High ventilatory minute volume
High minute volume reduces the FiO2 provided. Consider attaching supplementary O2.

#### Complications
- Hypotension
High CPAP values may decrease cardiac output
- Aspiration
- Barotrauma- Gastric distension.#### Equipment Required
- Flow-Safe II EZ CPAP Mask
- O2 Tubing- Oxygen source#### Procedure

- Ensure patient is monitored (ECG, BP and SpO2).	

- Connect O2 tubing to O2 source.

- Place the face mask securely to the patient’s face using head harness, adjust straps as required.

- With nebuliser in the OFF position (Figure 1), increase gas flow 8L. 

- Check face mask fit to patient and device connections for leaks, adjust as required.

- Adjust the flowmeter until desired pressure is obtained.
Flow of 12 - 15 LPM is required to reach CPAP pressure of 8.5 - 10 cmH2O.

#### Increasing FiO2

- Identify/retrieve additional O2 source.

- Remove one black “nipple” from the front of the mask.

- Using standard O2 tubing attach O2 source to mask.

- Titrate flow rate of secondary O2 source (max. 15 LPM) to achieve desired SpO2.

- Steps 7 – 10 may be repeated with the other black “nipple” and a third O2 source if higher FiO2 is required.

#### Use of MDI Medications

- Ensure green nebuliser flow switch is off (Figure 1)

- Attach inline MDI adapter and administer MDI bronchodilators

#### Use of Nebuliser

- To activate nebulizer, rotate knob to ON position (Figure 2).

- If necessary, readjust flowmeter to obtain desired CPAP pressure.
Nebuliser use halves the amount of pressure able to be generated. 

#### Use of EtCO2

- Separate mask and CPAP device.

- Insert standard EtCO2 microstream line between mask and CPAP device and reassemble.

- Connect EtCO2 microstream line to Zoll monitor and press the CO2 button on the Zoll monitor.
Capnometry is unreliable via the CPAP device. Readings will typically be lower than expected although trend direction can be trusted. EtCO2 monitoring with the CPAP is primarily to guard against unrecognised apnoea and to provide accurate ventilation rate assessment.

#### Specific Notes
- Pressure relief limits maximum CPAP pressure to 25 cmH2O @ 25 LPM. 
- Manometer accuracy: 
- 2O = accuracy \xb13 cmH2O
- > 15 cmH2O = accuracy \xb15 cmH2O>

- A well-sealed mask is required to achieve desired CPAP pressure- **Respiratory Distress** is abnormal respiratory rate or effort. The patient is near normal consciousness and is working hard to breathe but can maintain normal EtCO2 values, while **Respiratory Failure** is inadequate oxygenation and/or an increase in EtCO2 regardless of the patient’s breathing workload.
- At higher tidal volumes and respiratory rates (i.e. high flow demand), the FiO2 reduces as additional room air is inspired 
- Additional O2 may be delivered via the ports as described above.
- Alternatively, an NRB may be more effective at delivering the required FiO2.

##### Type
A naturally occurring gas 

##### Presentations
Compressed gas in all-white cylinders of various sizes, to be fitted with an appropriate pressure-reducing device 

##### Actions
Essential element for aerobic metabolic needs and sustaining life. 
 Oxygen administration improves, or prevents a reduction in, the oxygen content of blood leaving the lungs. 

#### Uses
A wide range of situations, for the treatment or prevention of hypoxaemia

#### Adverse Effects

- Increased risk of fire / explosion – oxygen strongly supports combustion 

- Mucosal dryness and irritation

- Hypoventilation in some COPD patients with CO2 retention (titrate oxygen to maintain SpO2 88 – 92%)

#### Contraindications

- Known paraquat poisoning with SpO2 ≥88%

- History of bleomycin therapy with SpO2 ≥88%

#### Precautions
Neonates (especially pre-term infants) are particularly susceptible to the toxic effects of oxygen. When absolutely required, the lowest effective concentration, for the shortest possible time, should be used to achieve adequate oxygenation.

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric
With the exception of conditions listed below, oxygen should generally be titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥94% (i.e, unless otherwise required, there is no need to push saturations to 100%). 

Deliver oxygen through a device appropriate to the required inspired concentration of oxygen.

- **Nasal cannulae: **1 – 4 litres/min (approx. 24 – 40% oxygen) for low oxygen requirements
- **Oxygen (‘Hudson’) mask: **6 – 15 litres/min (approx. 30 – 65% oxygen) for moderate oxygen requirements
- **Nebuliser: **8 litres/min for moderate oxygen requirements with need for aerosolised medications- **Non-rebreather mask (NRBM): **15 litres/min (approx. 60 – 97% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Bag-valve-mask (BVM): **15 litres/min or demand valve (up to 100% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP): **8 – 15 litres/min (titrated to achieve desired effect/pressure) for high oxygen requirements.
#### Conditions with specific oxygen requirement
- Conditions requiring 100% oxygen delivery (NRBM, BVM) 
- Obstetric emergencies
- Carbon monoxide poisoning
- Unrelieved upper airway obstruction
- Diving emergencies.
- COPD patients with possible CO2 retention – titrate oxygen delivery to maintain SpO2 of 88 – 92%.
- Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) – aim for highest SpO2 possible 
- High flow oxygen (15 litres/min) via nasal cannulae is mandatory during this procedure, in addition to IPPV.

#### Indications
- For patients that require supplemental oxygen.

#### Procedure

Figure 1

##### Nasal Prongs (minimal oxygen requirements – approx 24-40%)

- Attach nasal prong tubing to the oxygen port.

- Select oxygen flow rate. 
- **Note: 15 Lpm continuous during intubation.**

- Place one nasal prong in each nostril and tubing tucked behind each ear.

- Position the slide adjuster underneath the patient’s chin.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 2

##### Hudson Mask (moderate oxygen requirements – approx 30-65%)

- Attach the oxygen tubing to the Hudson mask.

- Connect the oxygen tubing to the oxygen port.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the metal nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 3

##### Non-Rebreather Mask (high oxygen requirements - approx 60-97%)

- Prepare as for Hudson mask.

- Select oxygen flow rate of >10 Lpm.

- Ensure reservoir bag is inflated.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.
